description: An argument is 'kithela' when its premises entail its conclusion under the stated reading. The judgement concerns the argument's structure, not the truth of its premises or the speaker's confidence. A perfectly valid path can begin from a false premise and arrive exactly where that premise leads.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into high-front unrounded 'i'. Place the tongue tip at the upper teeth for stressed unvoiced 'th', then open into middle-height front 'e'. Touch the tongue tip at the alveolar ridge for lateral 'l' and finish on central 'a'.
usage_notes: The adjective appears before an argument noun or predicatively; the quality-noun rule supplies validity. 'shewo' (true) assesses a proposition, 'telua' (accurate) assesses correspondence or execution, and 'norethi' (confident) reports strength of stance.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Validity separates structural relation from attachment to a preferred conclusion. Form and factual premises can then be examined independently.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A valid argument may have false premises, and an invalid one need not condemn its speaker. Logical assessment stays with the reasoning.

semantic_domains.cognition: the quality of an argument whose premises entail its conclusion
semantic_domains.wisdom: a precise assessment kept distinct from truth and persuasion
